Proposal number: 1333903

Proposal: Enviro Commons

Summary
The platform is built and viewable only by request. Below are some of the big picture influences: 

1. Maximizing climate change action impact lies in establishing action as a natural component in every part of our lives, not just 1 more thing to do. 

2. We experience perceptual barriers and a sense of powerlessness around them. All sectors have barriers to action. People can't always see these barriers - it's like fish not experiencing water.  Technology is likely the only way to overcome them.

Here are some macro-level or systemic barriers to action:

context for action
action support system
tools for action
 

3. Here are some of the personal-level barriers to action:

We are overwhelmed by the complexity & the quantity of problems. Platform simplifies, guides & supports so we can each do our share.
We cannot see what others are doing. Platform exposes action in ways to see your impact and the difference your actions make. You can see the bucket and the drops being added to it.
People feel powerless to influence others to take action.  Platform is essentially an 'influence system'. 
Balancing present & future needs favors the present. It's important to think of action within the context of a person's life given that much of our individual resources are used to just maintain or achieve a decent standard of living that what's left of our time and energy must be  used carefully. Platform provides life-improving benefits in the present that encourage actions that benefit the future. 
Think global, act local.  Platform enables both thereby increasing the degree of overall efficacy.
 

4. Shifting attitudes & behaviors involves addressing both the macro and personal components: 

 Address the perceptual barriers we experience in taking action.
Create a new context that gets people facing in the same direction.
Enable and support mass participation.
People need tools to make the changes.    
Provide people with support to make the changes. 
 

This proposal will cover all of these components.  

Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Project

What actions do you propose?
NOTE: This IP belongs to Hélène Julien and is protected by law. 

FACING THE SAME DIRECTION

Integrating action into all areas of our lives so that action becomes a simple part of the fabric of life and we become people who just naturally take care of our planet.  Getting a mass of people to face the same direction becomes the result of visibly spreading action in the following ways:

Engaging people in action across as many roles we play in life as possible (homeowner, employee, experts, business owner, investor, parent, teacher, elected official, environmental advocate, etc.)
Engaging all sectors (government, business & non-profits) in action
Varying messaging about action across worldviews
Blue – life is a test:  ex God-loving Trump followers
Orange – individualists : life is a game:  ex Trump
Green – humanity is a family: tree huggers
Yellow – life is a system: believing in inter-connectedness of all life
 

THE CONTEXT

The context is structured as a national platform with 3 components where each category of participant e.g. individuals, governments, businesses & both environmental & other non-profits are able & empowered to:

DO THEIR SHARE
WITNESS ACTION
INFLUENCE ONE ANOTHER
 

It's critical this all be in one central place on a per-country basis. Once 2 or more countries have platforms, an international section will be added so people can begin to see change at that level.

MASS PARTICIPATION

Like any platform, getting it started is the hardest part. The method chosen to achieve this is to use a city-first model that involves cities joining the platform and providing emergency notifications via email, text and calls.  You can combine any of these for any number of phone numbers and emails. One account can provide notifications to spouses, children, babysitter provided they're all for the same zip/postal code.  

When an individual signs up to receive the emergency notifications, they must provide their address including zip/postal code. They are then automatically entered to a neighborhood group based on that zip/postal code. Emergency notifications can be tailored by the government depending on their needs to go to a single zip/postal code, a group of them based on the first digits or to all of the members of all of the neighborhoods in their jurisdiction.

Cities already connect emergency services and provide it to the media. Providing it directly to individuals can have an impact and potentially save lives. Have you ever felt like you're the last person to hear the news about something important? Being the last to hear is not a good idea with emergency matters.

I believe that this is the easiest way to grow mass participation in Enviro Commons. With safety and security being a foundational element of Mazlo's needs hierarchy, it's nearly automatic human want. If you disagree, ask around.  

Engagement isn't expected to be an automatic thing in a neighborhood or any type of functionality on the platform. One of the most important influence techniques will be to send each individual a weekly email to inform and influence engagement.  See Weekly Email section below for more details (shown after functionality).  A lot of the information on the platform can be useful to people so it's possible to say which piece will draw people in the most.

 THE PLATFORM FUNCTIONALITY - TOOLS FOR ACTION

The platform has 4 Dashboards:

Individuals 
Government
Organization
ENGO  (environmental non-profits)
 

 

The 4 Dashboards are comprised as follows:

INDIVIDUALS:

People sign up for emergency notifications from their governments & are automatically added to a neighborhood group based on their zip/postal code. Four areas for action by individuals:

Neighborhoods for connection & collaboration
Personal Actions page with actions tools to help people reduce energy use, increase sustainability & save money
Personal Well-Being page with a survey for people to assess their well-being & results and courses/information to improve their lives. 
Circles - groups for connecting & collaborating on issues
Expert Profiles - with proof of expertise in an environment-related area: their name gets a special extra something so wherever they participate their words may carry more weight.
 

The Dashboard page also offers other functionality such as news, statistics, trends, etc.

GOVERNMENT DASHBOARD

Governments pay a monthly fee for a profile that enables them to add posts, events and polls on their profile & in the following 3 areas (gov selects if for people of 1 neighborhood, a group or all):

Send out emergency notifications by text, email and phone
Add content to neighborhood groups
Add content to the actions section
Add content to the well-being section
 

The Dashboard page includes search functionality, as well as the Open Ideas section that enables people to create ideas for any government in the country that has a profile on the platform. People can also visit individual government profiles to learn all kinds of things about the government's commitment and efforts in areas related to climate change.

ORGANIZATION DASHBOARD

@Work Chapters for sharing ideas based on roles such as Network Managers, Libraries, Office Managers, Business Owners, etc or location- based sustainability groups.
Free Business Profiles that show what they're doing
Free Non environmental non-profits Profiles that show what they're doing
Paid Organization Sustainability Plans for them to do their share.  Ideal for small and medium businesses as well as larger ones to support their staff across many locations to do their share for the organization's sustainability. 
Free Organization Sustainability Plans for all non-profit organizations.  They can include 3 components: organization sustainability actions, personal actions and community collaboration tools. Ideal for religious and educational organizations and clubs.
Another future income stream that supports climate action. (Not shared information)
 

ENGO DASHBOARD

Free ENGO Profiles with proof of status.
They can create content such as projects, news, project results, organization results, newsletters, organization financial statistics and more.
The Dashboard page provides random projects, organizations, news, etc with every page load so people learn about many organizations and projects. 
 

The purpose of this model for the ENGO Dashboard is to deliver more than our current silo structure does where people only know about organizations and projects big enough to have marketing budgets. People can also search based on location and topic.

 

WEEKLY EMAIL

As mentioned above, engagement isn't going to be automatic just because they joined the platform and were automatically added to a neighborhood group.  Each individual will receive a weekly email that will contain several things such as:

Information on other ways to support their security such as emergency planning at the neighborhood and personal levels through the Emergency Preparedness Personal Action
Digest of their neighborhood updates for them to learn about their neighbors and neighborhood (if they're brand new neighbors) and other content such as events, wiki (for a neighborhood history perhaps!), posts, etc.  Maybe learn about a walking group, household jobs such as mowing, snow removal, etc.
Circles and @Work Chapters in your physical areas as well as keep up-to-date on content in your groups
ENGOs with projects in their areas - learn and keep up-to-date
Local businesses' profiles - learn
Governments can create events, polls and posts specifically for weekly emails in addition to selecting posts in other parts of the website
Digest of their governments' (municipal, province/state) Open Ideas
Enviro Commons will post platform growth statistics that can overt time influence people to be a part of something bigger than themselves
Emergency notifications are repeated here
Platform News
Highlighted user stories
Any content on the site you have chosen to follow 
 

 

INFLUENCE

It's worth noting that there was a time - in particular during the TV era of the 1970-2000 that it was believed that people were passive consumers of entertainment and not overly creative.  However, given the opportunities that became present since the 1990's w- particularly with the web, it is now believed that people are creators.  This is obvious by the huge growth in content creation on the web, explosion in groups on Meetup  and the increase in new companies and self-employment.  People want to create, participate and connect.

How much impact this has on participation on Enviro Commons in unknown therefore some influence is being built into it to support its growth.

As mentioned previously, the platform is in a sense an influence system.  Most of it is built in to the platform itself   and some is leadership driven. Bwlow are some of the influence 'tactics' built into to the platform. Please note that none of them are negative pressure in any way but occur naturally through social psychology aspects of groups:

Platform provides a focus & Measures change
Platform encourages vital behaviors by:
Creating a sense of solidarity and sharing a responsibility within a country based on their share as published by the UN I believe. Calculations for each country’s share is x decrease which equates to x in tons of energy.  Each person’s share involves making choices that add up to 15% decrease. Actions cover many areas such as:
Building
Transportation
Food
Purchasing adjustments
Helping people discuss important topics including the "undiscussables" such as “why should I do my share, when...".  The platform could use the Personal Dashboard's Enviro Commons'  blog feature for such topics. Also invite guest bloggers to contribute.
Platform engages all 6 sources of influence
Personal motivation – make the undesirable desirable - ex: a moral quest, stories, experiences, use gaming to make it a quest
Helps people do what they can’t – surpass their limits – ex: mindsets, skills, emotional skills
Provides encouragement – harness peer pressure
The power of seeing someone else take action
Seeing someone else sacrifice
Engage opinion and formal leaders
Make the "undiscussable" discussable
Provides assistance
When we can’t succeed on our own (groups). we can use some support
Blind spots (networking generates ideas)
Solidarity – create it to counteract the tragedy of the commons
Changes the economy (of inaction)
Remove the barriers (we use it like a currency to enable inaction)
Incentives (it's cool)
Reward behaviors and results (there's value in creating community connections  - particularly in situations of natural disasters, socializing, friendships, etc.)
Impetus that fear creates as we experience more disasters (timing)
Change the environment – remove the barriers to action, comparison between countries’ results, be transparent about data & results
 

 

Who will take these actions?
The Enviro Commons platform is a user-generated content model. Individuals and organizations maintain their own profiles and content. 

Enviro Commons' role is as follows:  

Enviro Commons would need a head office for business and technology functions and a professional services team that would be responsible for growing the platform worldwide as well as replicating and customizing it for countries.  

The growth plan used by the professional services team might be to work with 2-3 cities in a country to set up the platform with them and market it to their citizens.  The team would create a complete set of materials for other cities to set up their profile themselves and market to their citizens once they've signed up for the monthly software-as-a-service (SaaS). The cost of Government Profiles depends on its' population and average income of the citizens in the city.

Enviro Commons head office would create shared content such as webinars and videos to help organizations engage their employees, videos that amuse and encourage individuals to take action, videos to be run in meetings in neighborhood groups for them to collaborate and support one another on action, host webinars with expert speakers across @Work Chapters and videos for use by governments to run in large community meetings to kick start engagement.

NATIONAL SETUP

Enviro Commons would employ a team in each country to support engagement of all participation in that country.  The team would handle the moderation of content and issues related to its' user base with the help of a shared database of solutions.  For example - how to deal with warring neighbors or ideas on how to kick start action in a neighborhood where nothing is happening. In addition, the national team would use all of the available materials such as videos and webinars provided by head office to help engagement.

FLOW OF ACTION

How the waves of action flow:

So the first wave is governments providing emergency notifications through the Enviro Commons system and then those people being added to neighborhood groups. They start small and grow through adoption and influence techniques.

Groups begin to pop up in other areas and people join. Leadership grows.

Sustainability groups pop up at work because businesses feel they need to create profiles to stay in competition and they need to do their share.

People check out the ideas for their governments and soon they are curious on a regular basis.

People do the well-being survey and see ways to improve their lives. They also learn how to use the platform to do so.

People look up what companies are doing and they use the information for employment, buying and investment decisions.

People check out ENGOs and learn more about their projects because they can actually join & stay connected online. They can compare ENGOs and projects as well and learn about other organizations/projects they didn't know existed because of how the ENGO Dashboard refreshes content with every view. 

Where will these actions be taken?
As a national and replicable platform, it can be adapted to any country that sees it has value to them. The push for growth can come from individuals wanting their governments to adopt it as well as governments that want it.

To start I would begin with Canada for several reasons.

It's 1/10 the size of the US and therefore a little easier to handle the first time around.
The acceptance of the problems around climate change is higher in Canada than the US.
Greater disparity in worldviews in the US than Canada.
Politics are not as highly charged as is currently the case in the US.
Adoption in the US might be higher if 'keeping up with the Jones' effect were an influence factor.
 

The platform isn't limited to specific geographic areas however the priority is to establish it in countries that have the greatest impact on climate change.

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
No country selected

Country 2
No country selected

Country 3
No country selected

Country 4
No country selected

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
IMPACTS 

Adaptation would be greatly impacted through collaboration in neighborhood groups and sustainability actions. The well-being section can incorporate adaptation strategies as well. Even if people don't participate in the group, they still receive weekly emails with updates on their neighborhood, government content as well as statistics on results and more.     

 The Personal Actions section enables people to see the actual energy impact that their actions have such as the kilowatt savings of 1 light bulb, lowering the temperature on a water heater, etc.  They are able to quantify the total of their actions and translate it into dollar savings. Governments can also provide actual average energy usage based on zip/postal code to compare and goals to encourage.

For organizations, sustainability plans are part of stage 2 & are like 'neighborhood groups' for business.  (They have posts, polls, events, documents & wikis content) They would select their actions based on 'action sets' such as a 'restaurant set' or small office type business & customize the actions & the project management processes around the plan to meet their needs. They can engage all of their staff in their plan.  This is a way to engage the small to medium sized businesses and non-profit organizations that don't need robust and detailed plans. The fact is that there are a lot more small under-served businesses.

If you think one step beyond this sustainability plan and create a 'religious organization action set', add personal actions combine the 'community' functionality inherent to neighborhood groups, you create a way to engage Americans who are religious. The pope and other religious leaders are pushing for action and the plan provides a way for congregations to connect as a community with common goals, take personal action as well as collaborate on the sustainability of their organization. 3 wins in 1!  

This combines adaptation, emissions reductions and increased well-being.  There isn't space to show all the possibilities here but here's another:  large organizations can use it to push their sustainability actions to branches and geographic office locations.

Because the platform is an influence system, the focus switches to organizations showing what they are doing for people and planet through their profiles and content. It's not about their products but the value they bring to society.  The fact that a mass of people would have access to this perspective could have a huge impact on business sustainability and GHG emissions. 

There's another important indirect influence and that is that of the development of leadership. @Work Chapters are designed to expand knowledge of business sustainability through groups. Circles grow action around specific environmental issues. In both cases, these groups are the place to grow the leadership of experts, non-experts and environmental non-profit leaders. Collaboration impacts adaptation and GHG reductions through the sharing of knowledge.

What are other key benefits?
An important benefit would be an increase in the sense of personal power people experience to change the world. Because the underlying conversation is 'People and Planet First', the 99% have a greater ability to impact the world by helping them make better choices about businesses, governments and their own actions. This is simply empowerment.

The platform connects what may seem to some people as disparate pieces together because we're complex and we live in a complex world. We have to deal with our well-being, social goals, GHG reductions,  environmental action, sustainability changes, adaptation, open government so we know what's happening in our world, helping our employers engage in reduction action and continuous innovation and support non-profit organizations that tackle environmental issues that are dear to us  Dealing with them separately can lead  people to action as a bunch of to-dos in a big list.  The platform hopes to connect all of the disjointed pieces in a way so that things can flow together and be felt throughout every area of our lives in the way we live them. 

Consider this situation:  People in a region are experiencing a natural disaster.  They can use their online neighborhood connections, their free online non-profit church community (or other groups) and their work organization group to help each other find shelter, support, information.  Do people have this now?    Not only that, governments can get information to people directly through their neighborhood group as well as through the emergency notification system.  This becomes an extension to existing support networks that relies less on governments and more on each other.  As more and more disasters occur around the world, this will become even more important. It's the equivalent to giving people a 'fishing rod' to better help themselves.

 

Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
CURRENT STATUS 

A lot of the platform has been built therefore the costs to complete it aren't that high.  

IMPLEMENTATION ESTIMATE 

I estimate that a team of 5 people could get it off the ground and run it for the first country within a couple of months.

The biggest challenge is acquiring government participation.  Because each government must purchase a profile, there would be an immediate influx of cash at the time of launch. For future countries, there would be a separate professional services team for customization, training, co-marketing, etc. The cost could potentially be borne by the federal government of a country that wishes to have this software for its' citizens or it could be included in the monthly profile cost. Because of word-of-mouth and the globalization of the media the cost of the next country would be lower.  

Overall, I believe the platform could break even within 18 months.

NEXT STEPS

At this time, financial backing is required to continue the project. Because it's a social enterprise, a grant that supports the 'social' aspect would be ideal but venture funding is more likely. Government connections and business support are critical as well.

In terms of steps taken to date, I've found that few organizations are able to support this project within their 'scope'.  

So far I haven't been successful in creating the collaboration necessary to make this project happen.

 

CHALLENGES

Here a few: 

A flow between online and off-line action needs to be established. It's not enough to commit to doing something on your Personal Actions page so a support system needs to be there to help that happen.  Technology can enable this is a number of ways. Neighborhood groups can support action through Special Action Meetings that use videos.  The national support team would also so be instrumental in engagement support. 
Content moderation is also an important component of engagement support. Problems will crop up between users. I would like to employ a social psychologist to help devise techniques for the national teams to use and get involved as needed.
Platform security is another important challenge. Neighbors are easily able to flag people where the address is wrong, the number doesn't exist on the street or the person doesn't live at that address in order to protect themselves. Only a small amount of identifiable content is available to protect people.
Platform security risk is an ongoing concern & would require specialists.
Some people see Facebook as a competitor.  If you look at the business, non-profit and government perspectives, Facebook organization profiles are a 'push' of products, services and information to the public.  It's not sustainability-specific.  
Timeline
GROWTH PROCESS

The first 15 years would involve helping as many cities adopt the platform as possible.  Cities are the natural first step in launching within a country because: 

Cities already connect emergency services and provide it to the media.  
Cities are already committed to carbon reductions but most are doing it through infrastructure and sustainability projects. Few want to go into the weeds and deal directly with people so the platform handles that and the reduction numbers count towards the city's goals. 
Many cities are already providing information on well-being so the platform extends their capability to support people in improving their lives. 
Human issues are a growing component of cities' and society's load and the platform provides new capabilities to address them through neighborhood and community engagement. 
Open ideas in an open forum enables good ideas to cross and grow much faster. It's also a cheaper alternative along with controlling their own polls in neighborhoods to get feedback directly from the public.
 

IMPACT

The goal would be to expand the platform to many countries as quickly as possible to create a strong base of action. It would not work for just one government to set things up for themselves because they cannot address the barriers to the proper scale. According to studies on the perceived degree of the efficacy of initiatives, this factor has dogged environmental initiatives all over the world and reduced their ability to affect change.  Within each country, the impact would begin immediately and adaptation and community engagement would grow over time.  GHG emissions would start a little slower, rise as it expands over the sectors and influence grows.

In the medium and longer terms the rate of change would decrease but GHG reductions would continue to increase with new technology adoption. At some point in the future the goal changes to faster adoption rate of better technologies and getting that information out faster and to more people.  

The fact is that individuals and organizations reach a level where change drops off and picks up only with subsequent outside inputs like technological changes, moves, vehicle and appliance replacements, etc. 

Certain parts of the site would continue to grow - neighborhood connections, Circles' work on issues would continue forever, people always need to stay on top of things with @Work Chapters, governments and people will always have Open Ideas to discuss, etc.

OTHER NOTES

1. A mobile version of the platform will be created.

2. The government perspective is that they are getting a suite of services that help them connect to their citizens in ways that Facebook doesn't and probably never would. Governments receive services that can help them achieve their GHG reduction targets by supporting the citizens & organizations in their jurisdiction. 

3. The business perspective is to show customers, investors & employees their commitment to sustainability through a free profile.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'city-first' adoption model contingent on governments purchasing SaaS profiles to access emergency notification systems. This creates a critical causal gap: there is no empirical evidence that municipalities will divert public funds to a private platform for emergency alerts when robust, existing public infrastructure (e.g., FEMA, local 911 systems, municipal alert apps) already exists. The assumption that 'safety' is a sufficient hook to bypass bureaucratic procurement and privacy hurdles is highly speculative. Furthermore, the claim that a team of 5 can launch a national platform with complex moderation, security, and multi-stakeholder dashboards within 'a couple of months' contradicts standard software development lifecycles and security compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, PIPEDA) for handling sensitive location and emergency data.

novelty.rationale: The concept of gamifying sustainability and aggregating community action is not novel; it mirrors existing platforms like Nextdoor (neighborhood focus), Change.org (advocacy), and various carbon-tracking apps. The specific innovation proposed—segmenting messaging by worldview (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow) and integrating emergency alerts as a user acquisition funnel—is a novel *combination* of tactics. However, the underlying mechanism of 'influence' described (social proof, peer pressure) is well-documented in behavioral science literature, meaning the proposal offers a new application of known principles rather than a new scientific or technical mechanism.

impact.rationale: The proposal posits a direct causal link between platform engagement and significant GHG reductions ('15% decrease' per person) without providing a validated model for this conversion. The logic assumes that visibility of action and 'well-being' surveys will automatically translate into sustained behavioral change and measurable emissions cuts. This ignores the 'intention-action gap' well-documented in environmental psychology. Without a rigorous, evidence-based mechanism for how digital engagement scales to physical infrastructure changes (e.g., retrofitting homes, changing transport modes), the projected impact on global emissions remains theoretical and likely overstated. The reliance on 'influence' to overcome systemic barriers (cost, infrastructure) is a weak causal claim.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured logically with clear sections, but the presentation of evidence is weak. It relies heavily on rhetorical assertions ('Technology is likely the only way,' 'It's nearly automatic human want') rather than citing empirical studies or pilot data. The financial projections (break-even in 18 months) and timeline (15-year growth) are presented as facts without underlying sensitivity analysis or risk assessment. The tone is optimistic but lacks the precision required for a scientific or systems evaluation, particularly regarding the technical feasibility of the 'emergency notification' integration.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal presents a high-risk, low-probability pathway to impact. It conflates digital engagement with physical behavioral change and assumes a market demand (government purchase of emergency alert services) that contradicts current public sector procurement realities. The causal chain from 'signing up for alerts' to 'sustained GHG reduction' is tenuous and unsupported by the provided text. While the worldview segmentation is an interesting behavioral variable, the proposal fails to address the massive technical and institutional friction required to deploy such a system at scale.
key_concerns: The assumption that governments will pay for a private platform to deliver emergency notifications ignores existing public infrastructure and legal liabilities regarding data privacy and alert reliability.
No empirical data or pilot results are provided to support the claim that the platform can achieve a 15% reduction in individual energy use or that 'well-being' features drive climate action.

The timeline of 'a couple of months' for a team of 5 to build a secure, multi-tenant national platform with emergency alert integration is technically implausible given security and compliance requirements.
The proposal treats 'influence' as a sufficient substitute for structural economic and infrastructural changes required for deep decarbonization, which is a systems-level error.

The 'worldview' segmentation strategy (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow) is mentioned but lacks a defined mechanism for how these distinct groups will interact without causing polarization or platform fragmentation.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'city-first' adoption model driven by emergency notification utility, yet simultaneously demands that governments pay a monthly SaaS fee for this access. This creates a critical institutional contradiction: municipalities rarely allocate budget for 'marketing' or 'engagement' platforms under the guise of emergency services, and the proposal offers no mechanism to bypass procurement cycles or secure political buy-in for a paid, non-essential layer on top of existing emergency alert systems. Furthermore, the plan to employ national teams for content moderation and engagement in every country is fiscally unrealistic for a social enterprise without massive upfront capital, contradicting the claim that a 5-person team can launch a country.

novelty.rationale: The concept of a 'super-app' for sustainability that gamifies action and aggregates data is not novel; it mirrors existing platforms like JouleBug, Patch, or various municipal engagement portals. The specific 'influence system' framework (referencing 6 sources of influence) is a repackaging of established behavioral science (e.g., Vital Behaviors) rather than a new governance mechanism. The only distinct element is the explicit bundling of emergency alerts with climate action to drive adoption, but the governance model for this bundling is undefined.

impact.rationale: The proposal assumes that visibility of action and 'social pressure' will automatically translate into measurable GHG reductions, ignoring the 'intention-action gap' well-documented in policy literature. Without a regulatory mandate or a direct financial incentive (e.g., tax credits, utility rebates) integrated into the platform's core loop, the impact will likely remain limited to 'low-hanging fruit' behaviors (e.g., turning off lights) rather than systemic changes (e.g., retrofitting, transport shifts). The claim that the platform will 'change the economy of inaction' is a theoretical assertion with no evidence of a viable enforcement or incentive mechanism.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear headings and a logical flow from problem to solution, but it suffers from significant vagueness regarding the 'how' of governance. It relies heavily on abstract concepts ('face the same direction,' 'influence system') and psychological theories without detailing the operational, legal, or financial architecture required to sustain them. The tone is optimistic to the point of ignoring political friction, particularly regarding the 'Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow' worldview segmentation which, while theoretically sound, is politically explosive to implement in a single platform without deep local customization.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory standpoint, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism'—assuming a technological platform can solve deep-seated political and behavioral barriers without addressing the institutional incentives required for adoption. The core flaw is the assumption that governments will pay for a platform that competes with their own communication channels, and that citizens will trust a private entity with emergency data without a robust public mandate. The model lacks a clear path to political legitimacy and financial sustainability.
key_concerns: The 'pay-to-play' model for government profiles is a non-starter for most municipalities; the proposal fails to explain how to overcome the 'free rider' problem where cities expect emergency alert integration to be a public good, not a SaaS product.

Data privacy and liability risks are underestimated: aggregating emergency alerts with neighborhood social networking creates a high-risk vector for doxxing, harassment, and data breaches, which could destroy public trust instantly.

The proposal assumes a 'neutral' platform can navigate polarized worldviews (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow) without becoming a political battleground; there is no governance framework for content moderation that satisfies such divergent groups.

The financial model is contradictory: it claims a 5-person team can launch a country while simultaneously requiring national teams for moderation and engagement, suggesting a severe underestimation of operational complexity and cost.

There is no mechanism for accountability: if the platform fails to reduce emissions or if emergency alerts are delayed, who is liable? The proposal treats the platform as a passive tool rather than an active governance actor.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'chicken-and-egg' adoption strategy that is operationally fragile. It assumes governments will pay for a SaaS profile to access a user base that only exists if the government pays first. The claim that a team of 5 can launch a national platform with emergency notification capabilities (requiring strict telecom compliance, data sovereignty, and 99.99% uptime) within 'a couple of months' is operationally naive. Furthermore, the 'city-first' model contradicts the stated need for a 'national platform' to function as an influence system, creating a fragmented rollout risk.

novelty.rationale: The concept of gamified sustainability and community dashboards is well-trodden (e.g., Nextdoor, local government apps, carbon tracking apps). The novelty lies in the specific 'emergency notification' hook to bypass initial engagement barriers, which is a clever distribution tactic, but the core functionality (profiles, action tracking, forums) is a standard aggregation of existing social and civic tech features rather than a technological breakthrough.

impact.rationale: The theoretical impact on GHG reduction is high if mass adoption is achieved, as it targets behavioral change across multiple sectors. However, the proposal lacks a concrete mechanism to convert 'awareness' and 'well-being' metrics into verified, measurable emissions reductions. The reliance on 'influence' and 'social pressure' without a robust verification or incentive structure (beyond vague 'coolness') poses a significant risk that the platform becomes a 'greenwashing' tool rather than a driver of actual decarbonization.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally disorganized, mixing high-level philosophy (Maslow, worldview colors) with disjointed feature lists. It fails to provide a clear financial model, offering only a 'break-even in 18 months' claim without unit economics, customer acquisition cost (CAC) estimates, or a detailed breakdown of the 'monthly fee' for governments. The tone oscillates between visionary manifesto and unproven startup pitch, lacking the rigor required for a scalable implementation plan.

A3.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to solve the 'last mile' of climate action by bundling it with emergency services, a high-stakes hook that creates a massive operational liability. While the psychological framing is sound, the implementation plan ignores the immense regulatory, technical, and sales hurdles of selling to governments while simultaneously building a consumer social network. The 'team of 5' estimate for a national emergency platform is a critical red flag for operational feasibility.
key_concerns: Regulatory Liability: Using emergency notification systems for a commercial/social platform creates severe legal and liability risks if alerts are delayed, misdirected, or if the platform fails during a crisis.

Sales Cycle Mismatch: The proposal assumes governments will pay for a SaaS profile immediately, ignoring the typical 12-24 month procurement cycles and budget constraints of municipal/government entities.

Data Privacy & Security: Aggregating address-level data for emergency alerts and neighborhood groups creates a high-value target for bad actors; the proposal mentions 'security' but offers no architectural details on encryption, data sovereignty, or breach protocols.

Adoption Barrier: The 'city-first' approach creates a fragmented user experience. If a user moves or interacts with a neighbor in a non-participating city, the 'influence system' breaks, reducing network effects.

Verification Gap: There is no mechanism described to verify that 'Personal Actions' (e.g., turning off lights) actually result in the claimed GHG reductions, making the impact metrics potentially unverifiable.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'city-first' hook using emergency notifications to drive mass adoption, but this assumes governments will pay for a SaaS platform to replace or augment existing emergency alert systems (which are often free or mandated). The text admits 'few organizations are able to support this project within their scope' and that the creator has 'not been successful in creating the collaboration necessary.' Furthermore, the requirement for a 'national team' in every country to handle moderation and engagement is a massive operational burden that contradicts the claim of a lean 5-person launch team. The friction of getting governments to pay for citizen engagement tools, rather than providing them as a public utility, is a critical failure point not addressed.

novelty.rationale: The concept of gamifying sustainability and connecting neighbors is not new (e.g., Nextdoor, various carbon footprint apps). The specific novelty lies in the 'influence system' attempting to bridge disparate worldviews (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow) and the integration of emergency alerts as a Trojan horse for climate action. However, the proposal claims to solve 'perceptual barriers' with technology, which is a common, often over-sold assumption in this sector. The segmentation by worldview is a theoretical novelty, but the execution plan lacks evidence that this specific taxonomy will actually work on the ground to change behavior.

impact.rationale: The proposal promises to 'maximize climate change action impact' by making it 'natural,' but the mechanism is weak. It assumes that receiving a weekly email or seeing a 'bucket fill up' will overcome the deep structural barriers of poverty, time poverty, and lack of resources mentioned in the text itself ('resources are used to just maintain... a decent standard of living'). The impact is speculative and relies on a 'trickle-down' behavior change model that ignores the reality that marginalized communities often lack the agency to act even when informed. The 'fishing rod' metaphor is appealing but ignores that many people don't have the water to fish in.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is comprehensive in scope but suffers from 'feature creep' and a lack of focus. It attempts to solve emergency response, well-being, business sustainability, religious engagement, and climate action simultaneously. The writing style is optimistic and abstract ('fish not experiencing water') rather than grounded in the gritty reality of implementation. It lists many 'influence tactics' without explaining the practical friction of deploying them. The claim that 'a lot of the platform has been built' is vague and unverified, raising skepticism about the actual state of the product.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down solution that misunderstands the daily reality of the people it aims to serve. It assumes that if we just make the data visible and the notifications frequent, people will change their lives, ignoring the crushing weight of economic survival and the deep distrust many communities have for government-led digital platforms. The 'emergency notification' hook is a double-edged sword: it may get people to sign up, but it risks turning a climate platform into a surveillance tool or a source of anxiety, potentially alienating the very communities most vulnerable to climate disasters.
key_concerns: The 'emergency notification' hook creates a high risk of privacy violations and community distrust, especially in marginalized neighborhoods where government surveillance is a lived trauma.
The assumption that governments will pay for this SaaS model is unproven; most municipalities are cash-strapped and rely on free or mandated emergency systems.
The proposal ignores the 'digital divide' and the fact that the most climate-vulnerable populations often lack reliable internet access or digital literacy to engage with complex dashboards.
The 'weekly email' engagement strategy is likely to result in high unsubscribe rates and 'notification fatigue,' failing to drive the sustained behavioral change required.
The plan to employ a 'national team' in every country for moderation and engagement is financially unsustainable and contradicts the lean startup narrative.

There is a dangerous gap between the 'Personal Actions' page and actual physical capability; telling a low-income family to 'reduce energy use' without providing the capital for retrofits is performative, not impactful.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'city-first' adoption model driven by government purchase of SaaS profiles, yet explicitly admits to a history of failure in securing collaboration ('So far I haven't been successful'). The assumption that governments will pay for a platform to manage citizen behavior, while simultaneously competing with free, ubiquitous platforms like Facebook for attention, ignores the high transaction costs of public procurement and the political risk of centralized behavioral monitoring. The 'emergency notification' hook is a strong value proposition, but the leap from emergency utility to sustained climate action participation is a massive behavioral assumption with no evidence of traction.

novelty.rationale: The concept of gamifying sustainability and creating neighborhood-level social accountability is not novel; it echoes failed attempts at 'social credit' style environmental tracking and existing community apps (Nextdoor, Patch) that struggle with engagement. The novelty lies in the scope of integration, not the mechanism itself.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a severe distributional risk: it assumes a 'one-size-fits-all' platform can serve disparate worldviews (from 'God-loving Trump followers' to 'tree huggers') without fracturing. In reality, a centralized platform for 'undiscussables' and behavioral monitoring is likely to be rejected by the very groups it needs to engage, or worse, become a tool for exclusion where only the 'Green' demographic participates, leaving the 'Blue' and 'Orange' sectors untouched. The claim of 'mass participation' ignores the digital divide and the fact that those most vulnerable to climate change often lack the resources or trust to engage with such a system. The impact is speculative and relies on a 'trickle-down' influence model that has historically failed to shift systemic behavior.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is verbose and repetitive, often restating the same 'barriers' and 'solutions' without providing concrete metrics or a clear logic model. While it attempts to be comprehensive by covering all sectors, this breadth dilutes the focus. The presentation of the 'influence system' is theoretically dense but practically vague, relying on abstract psychological concepts ('solidarity', 'fear') without detailing the operational safeguards against manipulation or the specific governance structures required to maintain trust across polarized groups.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to solve the climate crisis by building a centralized, state-sanctioned social platform that leverages emergency services to bypass political polarization. From a justice and fairness lens, this is deeply problematic: it conflates safety with surveillance, assumes a homogeneity of values that does not exist, and risks creating a two-tier system where only the digitally literate and politically aligned can participate. The 'influence' tactics described are essentially soft coercion, raising significant ethical concerns about autonomy and the potential for exclusion of marginalized voices who may not trust a government-linked platform.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes that 'emergency notifications' are a neutral hook, ignoring that marginalized communities often have deep historical distrust of government surveillance and data collection.

The 'worldview segmentation' strategy (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow) is presented as a solution to polarization, but a single platform may inevitably become dominated by one worldview, effectively silencing or alienating others, thus exacerbating exclusion.

The financial model relies on government SaaS fees, yet the proposal admits to zero success in securing government buy-in; this suggests a fundamental misalignment with public sector priorities and procurement realities.

The 'influence system' relies on peer pressure and social comparison ('seeing the bucket and the drops'), which can lead to performative activism and shaming rather than genuine, equitable behavioral change.

There is no clear mechanism for ensuring that the 'well-being' and 'sustainability' actions are accessible to low-income populations who may lack the time, energy, or resources to engage with the platform's demands.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'city-first' adoption model driven by emergency notification utility, yet simultaneously demands a complex, multi-stakeholder SaaS infrastructure (government, business, ENGO, individual dashboards) to be built and maintained. The assumption that a team of five can launch a national platform with real-time emergency integration, content moderation, and security protocols within 'a couple of months' is critically optimistic and ignores the bureaucratic inertia of government procurement and the technical debt of building a secure, scalable civic platform. The financial model (governments paying for profiles) contradicts the 'social enterprise' nature and the reality of municipal budget cycles, creating a high risk of immediate stall.

novelty.rationale: The concept of gamifying sustainability and aggregating community action is not novel; it echoes failed attempts at 'social credit' style environmental tracking and existing community engagement platforms (Nextdoor, civic tech tools). The specific innovation of using 'emergency notifications' as a Trojan horse for climate behavior change is a clever tactical pivot, but the underlying mechanism—using fear/safety to drive long-term behavioral shifts toward sustainability—has a mixed historical record and risks creating a 'compliance' culture rather than genuine ecological resilience.

impact.rationale: While the long-term vision of a 'national fabric' of action is ambitious, the proposal fails to demonstrate a causal link between 'visibility of action' and actual systemic decarbonization. It risks creating a 'participation theater' where users feel empowered by tracking small, low-impact behaviors (e.g., changing a lightbulb) while ignoring the structural barriers to deep decarbonization (industrial policy, energy grid overhaul). The reliance on 'influence tactics' and 'social pressure' to drive change is fragile; without binding regulatory or economic shifts, the platform may simply shift the burden of climate action onto individuals who are already resource-constrained, potentially exacerbating social inequity rather than building resilience.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is comprehensive in scope but suffers from a lack of critical focus and rigorous systems thinking. It attempts to solve 'everything' (well-being, emergency response, business sustainability, political engagement) simultaneously, which dilutes the core value proposition. The language oscillates between high-level systems theory ('fish not experiencing water') and vague implementation details ('a team of 5'). The timeline of 15 years for global expansion is presented without a clear roadmap for the first 24 months, making the long-term vision feel disconnected from the immediate operational reality.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to engineer a societal shift through a digital platform that leverages emergency services as an entry point for climate action. From a long-term resilience perspective, it is dangerously optimistic about the ability of 'soft' influence and visibility to overcome hard structural barriers to sustainability. It risks building a fragile system that depends on continuous user engagement and government buy-in, both of which are historically volatile, rather than embedding resilience into physical infrastructure or binding policy. The 'emergency hook' is a double-edged sword that may erode trust if the platform is perceived as a surveillance tool or if the promised safety benefits do not materialize during actual crises.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes that 'emergency notifications' will seamlessly convert into long-term climate action without addressing the psychological disconnect between immediate survival needs and long-term sustainability goals.

There is a critical gap in the business model: why would cash-strapped municipalities pay for a SaaS platform when free alternatives exist, and how does this scale without creating a two-tier system where only wealthy cities can afford 'resilience'?

The reliance on 'social pressure' and 'visibility' to drive behavior change ignores the 'tragedy of the commons' and the potential for 'greenwashing' where organizations perform sustainability for the platform's metrics without making real operational changes.

The proposal lacks a concrete strategy for the 'flow between online and offline action,' which is the single biggest point of failure for digital sustainability initiatives; without this, the platform becomes a digital echo chamber with no physical impact.

The timeline and resource estimates (5 people, 2 months) are fundamentally disconnected from the complexity of integrating with government emergency systems and ensuring data security for sensitive citizen location data.

This reinforces my low feasibility score; the proposal treats a high-stakes public safety function as a mere user acquisition funnel, ignoring the regulatory and ethical friction. 

Furthermore, A5 and A6's critique of the 'Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow' segmentation is crucial. While I noted the lack of a mechanism, they correctly point out that a single platform attempting to serve such polarized worldviews without a robust, neutral governance framework risks immediate fragmentation or becoming a tool for exclusion. The proposal assumes 'influence' can bridge these divides, but the evidence suggests it will likely exacerbate them. 

Regarding impact, the pool confirms that the 'intention-action gap' is not merely a behavioral hurdle but a systemic one. The proposal's reliance on 'visibility' and 'well-being' surveys to drive decarbonization ignores the capital and infrastructure constraints (A4, A6) that prevent action regardless of motivation. 

My initial scores remain robust. The proposal's core flaw is a 'solutionist' fallacy: assuming a digital layer can override deep-seated institutional, economic, and psychological barriers without addressing the underlying structural dependencies. However, I must sharpen the disagreement on the 'worldview segmentation' (Blue/Orange/Green/Yellow). While A5 views this as a potential source of exclusion, I argue it is a fatal governance flaw. The proposal assumes a 'neutral' space can exist in a polarized political landscape, which contradicts the reality of modern digital governance. The 'influence system' described is essentially soft coercion, which lacks the democratic legitimacy required for public sector adoption.

Consequently, my confidence in the proposal's feasibility has solidified at the lowest end of the spectrum. The 'team of 5' claim is not just optimistic; it is a denial of the regulatory complexity involved in handling PII (Personally Identifiable Information) for emergency services. The proposal fails to address the 'who pays' and 'who is liable' questions, which are the primary gatekeepers for any public-facing digital infrastructure. The 'social enterprise' model is incompatible with the 'public utility' function it attempts to perform.

A4.text:

Furthermore, the claim that a team of 5 can launch a national platform with complex moderation, security, and multi-stakeholder dashboards within 'a couple of months' contradicts standard software development lifecycles and security compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, PIPEDA) for handling sensitive location and emergency data.

The logic assumes that visibility of action and 'well-being' surveys will automatically translate into sustained behavioral change and measurable emissions cuts. The tone is optimistic but lacks the precision required for a scientific or systems evaluation, particularly regarding the technical feasibility of the 'emergency notification' integration.
